faqs.q: What does it mean to be outside your window of tolerance?
faqs.a: Moving above the top edge leads to hyperarousal, which can feel like panic, racing thoughts, anger, or the urge to fight or flee. Dropping below the bottom edge leads to hypoarousal, which can feel like numbness, fog, exhaustion, or going blank and disconnected.

faqs.q: Can your window of tolerance change?
faqs.a: Yes. The width of the window varies from person to person and from day to day. It can shrink after poor sleep, illness, or a stretch of high stress, and trauma can narrow it so that smaller triggers push someone out of it.

faqs.q: How do you get back into your window of tolerance?
faqs.a: Grounding and regulation skills are commonly used to come back when arousal climbs too high or drops too low. Many people find these skills easier to use once they can notice their own edges, which is part of why the model is taught in therapy.
